rows.answer: No. A harmonic (1,1) form represents a class in \(H^{1,1}(X) \cap H^2(X, \mathbb{R})\), but "rational Hodge class" would imply it lies in \(H^2(X, \mathbb{Q}) \cap H^{1,1}(X)\). Without knowing it is rational, the caption cannot claim it is a rational Hodge class.

Yes, by the Lefschetz (1,1) theorem and the Hodge conjecture for fourfolds, a rational (2,2)-class in \( H^4(X, \mathbb{Q}) \cap H^{2,2}(X) \) is algebraic, meaning it can be represented by an algebraic cycle. The "register ladder" (likely referring to the Hodge conjecture framework) ensures this correspondence.

In P^n, the codimension-k linear subspace P^{n-k} is the **body**, the projection (shadow) onto a complementary subspace is the **shadow**, and the **theorem-defined direction** is the kernel of the projection, i.e., the direction along which the body is projected to form the shadow. This is formalized by the projection theorem in projective geometry.

Yes, a first Hodge diamond for a smooth projective curve \( C \) of genus \( g \) should use \( H^1(C, \mathbb{C}) \) as the primary shadow, since it carries the Hodge decomposition \( H^1 = H^{1,0} \oplus H^{0,1} \), reflecting the curve's complex structure. The algebraic body here is the Jacobian variety \( \text{Jac}(C) \), which parametrizes line bundles of degree 0 and is a direct consequence of \( H^1 \)'s structure.

rows.target_register: R3->R4 in the known p=1 case
rows.answer: The answer key can name the **Néron-Severi group** (or its image in cohomology), as it classifies rational (1,1)-classes up to numerical equivalence. It does **not** generalize to higher-degree classes (e.g., (2,2)-classes), where the Néron-Severi group is insufficient and one must consider the full **Griffiths group** or **intermediate Jacobian** obstructions.

No, a rational (1,1) class on a smooth projective surface is not a new Hodge phenomenon beyond divisors. By the Lefschetz theorem on (1,1)-classes, every (1,1) class in the Néron-Severi group (which is the group of divisors modulo numerical equivalence) is the first Chern class of a line bundle, hence corresponds to a divisor. Thus, rational (1,1) classes are already accounted for by divisors.

Yes, by the Lefschetz theorem on (1,1)-classes, a rational (2,2)-class on a smooth projective threefold is represented by an integral curve class, as the integral cohomology of the Picard group injects into the rational cohomology. Thus, the rational class can be upgraded to an integral one.

Yes, the generator can reuse the smooth-projective Hodge cardinal (Hodge conjecture) without altering the source row, as the Kähler assumption ensures the (p,p)-class admits a harmonic representative, aligning with the Hodge decomposition. The rational class's existence in the image of the cycle map (modulo homological equivalence) preserves the structure, allowing reuse of the projective framework.

Yes, the algebraicity of the locus where a fixed class stays of type (p,p) implies the existence of an algebraic cycle representing that class, by the Lefschetz (1,1) theorem. This theorem ensures that such classes are precisely those coming from algebraic cycles, thus constructing the algebraic cycle body.
The highlighted region should be labeled as "a cycle" rather than "the cycle," since the (1,1) class contains many homologous cycles, not just one.
